title: The mid-2026 DeFi map
subtitle: Where capital sits, where it moves, and what the on-chain data actually says about the second half of the cycle.
author: DefiLlama Research
seoDescription: Where we are Halfway through 2026, on-chain capital allocation has split cleanly into three lanes: yield-bearing collateral, stablecoin liquidity, and execution
excerpt:
Where we are
Halfway through 2026, on-chain capital allocation has split cleanly into three lanes: yield-bearing collateral, stablecoin liquidity, and execution-tier infrastructure.

plainText:
Where we are
Halfway through 2026, on-chain capital allocation has split cleanly into three lanes: yield-bearing collateral, stablecoin liquidity, and execution-tier infrastructure. Each lane has a clear leader, a credible challenger, and a long tail. This piece walks through where the deposits actually sit — and which numbers we think are worth tracking through the rest of the year.
Total TVL across DeFi sits near $182B at the time of writing, up roughly 14% YTD. Lido remains the single largest pool. Aave is the largest lender on Ethereum. What changed most this cycle? Where new deposits land — not how big the pie is.
Charts and embedded dashboards in this piece pull from the DefiLlama API on render. If a number looks stale, the daily snapshot hasn't ticked over yet — refresh in a minute.
Liquid staking and the long Lido shadow
Liquid staking is still the largest sector in DeFi by TVL, and Lido accounts for the bulk of it — though its share of staked ETH has slipped from ~32% at the 2024 peak to roughly 27% today as native and exchange-staked ETH grew faster. The chart below tracks total deposited ETH, valued in USD.
[Chart: Lido]
Three forces are shaping what comes next:
Restaking saturation. Yield premia have compressed as more vaults compete for the same operator slots. The marginal AVS now pays less than the marginal validator earns from beacon-chain rewards alone.
L2 settlement migration. More LST liquidity is moving off mainnet into rollup-native wrappers — wstETH on Base is up nearly 4× year-over-year.
Issuer concentration. The top three issuers still control the majority of LST validator share. Worth watching, even if Lido's individual share is shrinking.
Quick number: LSTs and LRTs combined still represent roughly 36% of all DeFi TVL — more than lending, DEX liquidity, and bridges combined.
Issuer share, ranked by TVL contribution as of last weekly snapshot:
Issuer

The interesting movement isn't at the top. It's the cluster of mid-cap issuers (Mantle, Frax, Stader) collectively gaining share at the expense of Rocket Pool and centralized stakers. Below is the announcement that kicked off the rebalance:
[Embed: DefiLlama on the LST issuer rebalance]
Lending: Aave still wins, but margins are thinning
Lending TVL on Aave has been remarkably steady through the cycle. The interesting story is on the fee side, where compressed spreads have started to flow through to protocol revenue.
[Chart: Aave V3]
If you assume liquidity is migratory, you're underestimating how much of it is just collateralizing the same loan, year after year.
Paul Frambot's writeup on Morpho's vault model is the cleanest framing of why the spread economics differ — worth reading before drawing conclusions from the table below:
[Embed: Vault economics in Morpho Blue, end-to-end]
A useful sanity check
When evaluating a lending market, fees / borrowed_supply is the single most useful ratio for protocol health. The discontinued metrics — deposits / TVL — were always noisier than they looked, and borrow utilization on its own misses the spread side entirely.
Snapshot of the top five lending markets, ranked by net deposits:
Protocol
Net deposits
Borrowed
30d fees

Aave V3

Morpho Blue and Fluid both run leaner than Aave on a fee-per-unit-borrowed basis — they pass more spread to lenders. Aave still wins on absolute revenue because the borrowing book is 5× larger. The structural question for the next quarter: does that ratio cross over for any major protocol?
Stablecoin flows
Tether continues to dominate circulating supply, but the share gap with USDC has narrowed every quarter since Q3 2025, and yield-bearing stables (sUSDe, sUSDS) collectively crossed $15B in supply for the first time in March. Where the supply lives is now as informative as how much there is.
[Chart: Ethereum]
Three observations from chain-level flow data over the last 90 days:
Solana net stablecoin inflows turned positive again in March after seven months of bleed — most of it driven by USDC native issuance, not bridged USDT.
Base remains the largest non-mainnet host of USDC liquidity. Native issuance on Base hit a fresh ATH of $3.6B.
Tron's USDT supply has plateaued for the first time in three years. Migration to Plasma and to USD1 on Tron is the obvious culprit.
For a longer read on why Layer-2 stablecoin issuance pulled away from mainnet this cycle, our Q1 sector recap walked through the issuer-by-issuer mechanics:
[Embed: Q1 2026 stablecoin recap: where issuance went]
Risk and what we're watching
Heads-up: two of the largest LRT vaults are now collateralized by overlapping operator sets. A single slashing event would touch both. We flagged this internally in March; the operator overlap has since increased, not decreased.
Hasu's Flashbots newsletter has been tracking restaking risk concentration since 2024. The April issue is the most thorough public framing of the operator-overlap problem we've seen:
[Embed: Restaking and the operator bottleneck]
Research backlog for the rest of the quarter:
Monitor LST/LRT issuer concentration weekly.
Track restaking AVS revenue dispersion.
Build dashboard for cross-chain stablecoin net flows.
Backfill historical lending utilization series across Aave V2/V3 forks.
Last quarter, in three sentences
Restaking yields halved. Lending fees compressed. Stablecoin issuance moved decisively to Layer 2s. Each of those was a thread we expected — but the speed surprised us.
Methodology
Every number in this piece is pulled directly from DefiLlama's public API. Aggregations and ratios are computed in-place; nothing is stored. Example query for protocol TVL:
curl -s https://api.llama.fi/protocol/lido \
  | jq '{tvl: .tvl[-1].totalLiquidityUSD, change_30d: .tvl[-1].totalLiquidityUSD / .tvl[-30].totalLiquidityUSD - 1}'
The full set of queries used to produce every chart and table in this piece — including the lending fee/borrowed ratios and the cross-chain stablecoin flow aggregations — is published as a gist:
[Embed: Mid-2026 DeFi map — query collection]
All values shown are USD-denominated and use end-of-day snapshots. For sector totals we sum across the public protocol list — categories are documented at defillama.com/categories. For a deeper walkthrough of how we aggregate, the team covered the full pipeline in this interview:
[Embed: SpaceX]

Liquid staking sector overview[1]. Lending sector overview[2]. Stablecoin dashboard and chain breakdowns[3]. Restaking AVS revenue series[4].
